name: Americium
name_origin: Named after the Americas, where it was discovered, by analogy with the naming of europium after Europe.

discovery.discoverer: Glenn T. Seaborg, Ralph A. James, Leon O. Morgan, Albert Ghiorso

occurrence.abundance_summary: Trace amounts have been detected in nature, formed by neutron capture in uranium ores, but americium is essentially a synthetic element produced in nuclear reactors and recovered from spent fuel.
states: trace in uranium ores (synthetic in practice)

compounds.name: Americium dioxide
compounds.summary: Black ceramic solid used as the active material in ionization-type smoke detectors and as a target for synthesizing heavier actinides.

compounds.name: Americium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Pink crystalline solid; used in fundamental coordination chemistry and in solution chemistry studies of the +3 actinides.

compounds.name: Americium(III) fluoride
compounds.summary: Pink solid used as a precursor for producing metallic americium by reduction with barium or lithium vapor.

uses: Active material in household ionization smoke detectors (Am-241)
Industrial gauges for measuring thickness of glass, paper, and rolled metal sheets
Neutron sources when combined with beryllium (Am-Be) for oil-well logging and material analysis
Gamma-ray sources for radiography and medical diagnostics
Proposed fuel for space exploration RTGs as a long-term alternative to plutonium-238
reactivity_summary: A silvery-white metal that tarnishes slowly in dry air. It is more malleable than uranium or neptunium and reacts with oxygen, steam, and acids. The +3 state is most stable in aqueous solution, although +4, +5, and +6 states are accessible.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Americium-243 has the longest half-life (7370 years), but Am-241 (half-life 432.2 years) is by far the most widely used isotope, produced in kilogram quantities from spent reactor fuel.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
